title: Partial Clone, Sparse Checkout, and Large-Repository Scale · History Workshop
summary: A pairing session predicts each repository-state transition before building the first large-repository data and working-set contract for partial clone, sparse checkout, and large-repository scale. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of large-repository data and working-set contract while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Explain promisor remotes, promised objects, filter specifications, lazy fetching, missing-object tolerance, and command compatibility.
Compare shallow clone, partial clone, sparse checkout, sparse index, single branch, and reference repositories by omitted data and semantic limits.
Use cone patterns, non-cone patterns, set, add, reapply, disable, skip-worktree state, and sparse index while detecting vivified paths.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-advanced cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model
steps.instruction:
workshop case — Case G93 is a workshop in a disposable repository. the failing build omits a required shared package from the declared cone. The learner must predict the active sparse paths expose the intended working set without claiming object completeness, run git sparse-checkout list, explain apps/status is present while packages/shared is absent from the build working set, test this changed condition: add packages/shared and repeat both sparse-index status and full-object release verification, and preserve this recovery boundary: retain the prior sparse specification and support disabling sparse checkout reversibly.

What must be established before implementation?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for explain promisor remotes, promised objects, filter specifications, lazy fetching, missing-object tolerance, and command compatibility.

steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact
steps.instruction: Append an Advanced Git evidence block headed "# Evidence: gita-scale-fallback-transfer-24mgyr" with exact before and after refs; a falsifiable prediction; the bounded simulator command; expected, observed, tree, behavior, changed-case, and recovery evidence; and one honest real-repository transfer limit.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Gita Scale Fallback Transfer into observable performance evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: gita-scale-fallback-transfer-24mgyr
# scenario-key: trace-24mgyr probe-rygm42 boundary-mgyr24 owner-24mgyrx fault-24mgyrz
# before-ref: main=a11ce24 candidate=b00b1yr
# hypothesis: the failing build omits a required shared package from the declared cone
git sparse-checkout list
# expected: the active sparse paths expose the intended working set without claiming object completeness
# observation: apps/status is present while packages/shared is absent from the build working set
# changed-case: add packages/shared and repeat both sparse-index status and full-object release verification
# after-ref: main=a11ce24 candidate=c0ffeer
# tree-proof: the affected tree and path set match the predicted repository transition
# behavior-proof: a changed input preserves the named product or release invariant
# recovery: retain the prior sparse specification and support disabling sparse checkout reversibly
# transfer-limit: full release and offline recovery need explicit promised-object and promisor-availability checks
